blog.title: 12,000 People Showed Up to Say You Can't Fit a Person in a Dictionary
blog.body:
Kia Ora Peers and Queers,

The Legislation (Definitions of Woman and Man) Amendment Bill is now reviewing submissions. Introduced by NZ First MP Jenny Marcroft, it would write one fixed meaning into law: "woman" means an adult human biological female and "man" means an adult human biological male, regardless of gender identity. We all know that his would have a huge impact because the definitions would ripple out across every Act we already have (Wikipedia; RNZ, June 2026).

When the public got a chance to have their say, they turned out. Submissions closed on 2 July, and an independent analysis by Emily Aung of the 12,050 published submissions found 89.4% opposed the bill: 10,708 people against, 1,265 in support (emaung.com/bill-submissions). Ninety-eight percent wrote in their own words rather than pasting a template, which took real mahi from a lot of people. Aung is careful to call it a best-effort estimate rather than an official tally, and even so, not even a Marmite sandwich Luxon could mistake the voice of Aotearoa.

There's been a problem with some submissions though. Parliament's rules say submissions with offensive language can be rejected or returned, which sounds reasonable written down. The worry in the rainbow community is about who gets to decide what counts as "offensive," and whether trans and takatāpui submitters writing honestly about their own lives got caught out for it. A Wellington group, Queer Endurance / Defiance, is gathering evidence about submissions the Select Committee rejected, to test whether valid ones were handled fairly. If yours was bounced, they want to hear from you (Instagram @queer_endurance_defiance, or queerendurancedefiance@gmail.com). No official numbers on the rejections have been published, which is part of why this evidence matters.

So if your submission came back rejected or returned, that's not the end of it. You can contact the committee to ask why, and you can report it to Queer Endurance / Defiance so it gets counted. The select committee stage isn't the finish line anyway. A second reading, a committee of the whole House, and a third reading are all still ahead, so the pressure people keep putting on this bill really does still count.

The rangatahi and whānau watching this are in good company. The New Zealand Law Society submitted that the bill should not proceed past select committee. The Human Rights Commission, Tika Tangata, said it isn't necessary and risks further harm to Rainbow people. Twenty-five rainbow organisations signed an open letter arguing it would make no one safer while putting every woman under suspicion (Scoop, June 2026).

This was boring law and legal story to sit alone in the beehive. Around 10,000 people protested across five cities on 13 June, and the everyday laws these definitions would touch are the same ones all of us live under here in Te Tau Ihu. If you're eleven and reading this and are worried, hold onto the fact that thousands of kiwis have already stood up in your corner, on paper and in the street. A big majority saying "No" doesn't guarantee the outcome, and feeling wobbly about that is completely valid. That uncertainty is also why the steps still to come are worth watching.

If this week is a rough one, you don't have to sit with it on your own. However this bill goes from here, far more people showed up for you than showed up to write the definitions. And if you are feeling low, you can reach us through the Q Youth contact page, or call OUTLine NZ on 0800 688 5463 or Youthline on 0800 376 633, whichever feels easier.
blog.author: Nate

blog.title: 250-plus schools chose aroha out loud this Pride Week
blog.author: Nate
blog.body:
Kia Ora Peers and Queers,

A snail is not the animal you'd pick if you wanted to look cool. It's slow, it leaves a trail, and it lives inside a shell it drags around everywhere it goes. But that shell is the whole point, and it's why InsideOUT Kōaro made the snail the mascot for Schools' Pride Week Aotearoa 2026 (held 15–19 June, theme "Community is Home"): wherever a snail ends up, its home is already with it.

The idea underneath that is a lovely one for takatāpui and rainbow people. You carry your home, your identity and your community with you, whether you're in a big city or a small regional town, which means you don't have to shift to Auckland or Wellington to find your people or to belong. For those of us in Te Tau Ihu, the top of the South, that's worth hearing, because your people are already here.

The thing that genuinely made us smile was the numbers. More than 250 schools and community groups took part in 2026 (Scoop News, 19 June 2026), spanning early childhood centres, kura, primary, intermediate and secondary schools, tertiary providers and community orgs, and a lot of them were doing it for the very first time. What makes that figure worth celebrating is that it's a rebound. Participation had slipped over the previous couple of years, worn down by a wave of anti-rainbow noise online, so having more schools turn up this year is communities deciding, on purpose and out loud, that aroha is the response after a rough stretch.

You might wonder why it matters that a school bothers to throw a bit of a party for its rainbow rangatahi. It comes down to safety. As Judy O'Brien, Chief Executive of InsideOUT Kōaro, put it, "Feeling safe, celebrated and valued at school is foundational to young people's wellbeing and learning." The gap is real, too: around 41% of rainbow students reported being bullied at school in the past year, compared with 23% of their classmates (Scoop News, 19 June 2026). When 250-plus schools decide to celebrate openly, they're doing more than decorating a hallway, they're telling their rainbow students they're safe and they're wanted.

Because Schools' Pride Week is nationwide, schools right across the motu, including here in the top of the South, were part of the same wave. Alongside it ran a letter-writing project where rainbow adults and young people wrote down affirmations, hopes and dreams for takatāpui, MVPFAFF+ and rainbow rangatahi. Chosen whānau and blood whānau both build a home, and this was that idea put into ink.

If you'd like to know more, or you want to get your school involved next time, have a kōrero with InsideOUT Kōaro (insideout.org.nz) or check the Schools' Pride Week hub (pride.school.nz). And if you're after something local, the Nelson Provincial Museum is running "OUR VISION: Rangatahi, Imagining the Future, Shaping Tomorrow," a Nelson Youth Council exhibition, from 11 September to 18 October 2026, free for Nelson Tasman locals. It isn't rainbow-specific, but it's a real celebration of young people shaping what comes next, right on our doorstep.

None of this makes home a postcode, and 250-plus schools turning up this year is a decent reminder that community is something people keep building wherever they are, slowly and stubbornly, one trail at a time.

If you ever want someone to talk to, we're here. Reach out through the Q Youth contact page at qyouthnz.com, or call OUTLine NZ on 0800 688 5463 or Youthline on 0800 376 633. Any time, any day.

blog.title: Welcome to the Q Youth blog
blog.body:
Kia ora! This is our new blog — a space where we'll share news, updates, and kōrero on topical issues that matter to rainbow rangatahi and their whānau in Te Tau Ihu.

Check back here for posts about what's happening at Q Youth, upcoming events, and issues affecting our community. If there's a topic you'd like us to cover, get in touch — we'd love to hear from you.
